content.aim: The 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline provides recommendations for the treatment of patients with atrial fibrillation.
content.methods: Literature search from May 12, 2022, to November 3, 2022, including studies and reviews from sources like PubMed, EMBASE, and Cochrane Library.
content.structure: The guideline updates previous recommendations and includes new guidance on atrial fibrillation, thrombombolic risk, anticoagulation, left atrial appendage occlusion, and risk factor modification.

content.guideline: 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
key_points: Flexibility in using clinical risk scores, such as CHA2DS2-VASc, for stroke and systemic embolism prediction.
Consideration of stroke risk modifiers for atrial fibrillation (AF) patients with an intermediate risk of ischemic stroke.
Importance of early rhythm control in managing AF patients.
Catheter ablation has been recommended as a first-line therapy in certain patients.
Recommendations for patients with device-detected AF and considerations for those with implantable devices.
Left atrial appendage occlusion devices have a higher Class of Recommendation due to new evidence.
Recommendations for patients with AF identified during medical illness or surgery to emphasize risk management.

Introduction.Methodology and Evidence Review: The recommendations listed in this guideline are, whenever possible, evidence-based. An initial extensive review of studies is conducted to ensure guidelines are founded on solid evidence.

content.Guideline Modification and Presentation: Guidelines are presented in a modular format that includes recommendations, synopses, and supportive text. Evaluations are continually updated based on new data and developments.
content.Cost-Value Considerations: Analysis of value for drugs and interventions is included when appropriate as per ACC/AHA methodology.
content.Writing Committee: Committee selections ensure diverse representation from the cardiovascular community and appropriate expertise.
content.Industry Relationships: The ACC and AHA maintain policies to ensure guidelines are developed without commercial influence.

Class of Recommendations and Level of Ev.Purpose: Indicates the strength of the recommendation and the estimated magnitude and certainty of benefit.

Comparative-Effectiveness Phrases: Treatment A is recommended/indicated in preference to treatment B
Treatment A should be chosen over treatment B
Treatment/strategy A is probably recommended/indicated in preference to treatment B
It is reasonable to choose treatment A over treatment B

Suggested Phrases: Usefulness/effectiveness is unknown/unclear/uncertain or not well-established

Level A: High-quality evidence from more than 1 RCT
One or more RCTs corroborated by high-quality registry studies
Level B-R (Randomized): Moderate-quality evidence from 1 or more RCTs
Level B-NR (Nonrandomized): Moderate-quality evidence from 1 or more well-designed, well-executed nonrandomized studies, observational studies, or registry studies
Level C-LD (Limited Data): Randomized or nonrandomized observational or registry studies with limitations of design or execution
Physiological or mechanistic studies in human subjects
Level C-EO (Expert Opinion): Consensus of expert opinion based on clinical experience

Notes: COR and LOE are determined independently.
A recommendation with LOE C does not imply that the recommendation is weak.
The outcome or result of the intervention should be specified.
The method of assessing quality is evolving, including the application of standardized, widely-used, and preferably validated evidence grading tools.

content.topic: Prevalence of Atrial Fibrillation (AF) Among Medicare Beneficiaries from 1993 to 2007.
highlights: AF diagnosis rates vary by education, income, clinical, and genetic factors.
Overall lifetime risk: 30-40% in White individuals, ~20% in African American individuals, ~15% in Chinese individuals.
AF is associated with increased risk of stroke, cognitive impairment, myocardial infarction, sudden cardiac death, heart failure, chronic kidney disease, and peripheral artery disease.
Mortality risk from AF may be higher in men than women.
In Medicare beneficiaries, AF outcomes within 5 years include death, heart failure, new-onset stroke, gastrointestinal hemorrhage, and myocardial infarction.

Definitions.AF: A supraventricular tachyarrhythmia with uncoordinated atrial activation and ineffective atrial contraction. Electrocardiography characteristics include irregular R-R intervals, absence of distinct P waves, and irregular atrial activity.
Definitions.Clinical AF: Term used for documentation and therapy of arrhythmia with evidence from randomized trials.
Definitions.Subclinical AF: Arrhythmia identified in individuals without symptoms and no previous ECGs documenting AF.
Definitions.Atrial high-rate episodes: Events exceeding the programmed detection time limit, needing visual confirmation.
Definitions.AF burden: Frequency and duration of AF, defined as durations of an episode or total duration during monitoring.
Definitions.First detected AF: First documentation of AF regardless of symptoms.
Definitions.Paroxysmal AF: AF that is intermittent and terminates within 7 days of onset.
Definitions.Persistent AF: AF that continues more than 7 days and requires interventions.
Definitions.Long-standing persistent AF: Continuous AF for more than 12 months.
Definitions.Permanent AF: AF not subjected to further rhythm control efforts, accepted as a permanent condition.

Terms considered obsolete.Definition: This historical term has had variable definitions and should be abandoned. It has been replaced by the 'paroxysmal,' 'persistent,' 'long-standing persistent,' and 'permanent' terminology.

The distinction between 'valvular' and 'nonvalvular' AF remains a matter of debate. Recent trials comparing vitamin K antagonists with non-vitamin K antagonist oral anticoagulants in AF were performed among patients with so-called 'nonvalvular' AF. These trials have all allowed native valvular heart disease other than mitral stenosis (mostly moderate and severe) and prosthetic heart valves to be included. We should no longer consider the classification of AF as 'valvular' or 'nonvalvular' for the purpose of defining the etiology of AF, since the term was specific for eligibility of stroke risk reduction therapies. Valvular and nonvalvular terminology should be abandoned.

This term has been used in the past to identify AF in younger patients without structural heart disease who are at a lower risk for thromboembolism. This term does not enhance patient care, is not currently used, and should be abandoned.

Types of Atrial Flutter and Macroreentra: Illustrates different types, including typical flutter, reverse typical flutter, scar MRT, lower loop F1, and LA MRT.

Mechanisms and pathways leading to atria: Disrupted Ca2+ cycling and ion channel remodeling
content.AF: Atrial Fibrillation is characterized by a chaotic, rapid (300-500 bpm), and irregular atrial rhythm. AF results from electrophysiological or structural abnormalities of cellular connections facilitating rapid and uniform impulse conduction. Electrical abnormalities during AF suggest a shared phenotype with genetic, environmental, and metabolic etiologies.

genes.context: NLRP3 inflammasome activation contributes to oxidative stress and inflammation, leading to atrial fibrillation.

Electrophysiological Mechanisms.Electrical Remodeling: The observation by Haissaguerre et al. that ectopic firing from PVs triggers AF revolutionized treatments. Features like increased resting membrane potential and cross myofiber orientation contribute to AF.
Electrophysiological Mechanisms.Calcium Mishandling: Calcium mishandling in remodeling raises intracellular calcium levels in the sarcoplasmic reticulum, potentially increasing AF risk. Inadequate sequestration or regulation by calcium/calmodulin ATPase can occur.

content.Triggers of AF: Patients with AF often have shorter effective refractory periods, leading to faster conduction, dispersion of repolarization, and favoring reentry.

Atrial Structural Abnormalities.Atrial Cardiomyopathy: Described as changes that affect the atria structurally, electrically, or in any manner leading to clinical events. Changes can be structural, architectural, and contractile.
Atrial Structural Abnormalities.Impact of Remodeling: Includes fibrosis, altered ion channel expression, and inflammation, contributing to AF vulnerability. Hemodynamic overload increases risk.

content.Upstream Pathways: These include oxidative stress, fibrosis, calcium handling, and others, increasing AF susceptibility. Pathways like RAAS are involved.

genes.context: Calcium mishandling in AF of sarcoplasmic reticulum calcium ATPase underlies sequestration of intracellular calcium between beats, and/or altered regulation of sarcoplasmic reticulum calcium ATPase by phospholamban and sarcolipin.

content.topic: Atrial Fibrillation (AF) and its association with the autonomic nervous system (ANS).
content.autonomic triggers: Efferent autonomic cardiac innervation showing increased adrenergic and cholinergic nerve activity leading to changes such as automaticity and delayed afterdepolarization.
content.autonomic remodeling: Structural changes including sympathetic and parasympathetic hyper-innervation with electrical fractionation.
content.AF-associated autonomic dysfunction: Involves afferent autonomic cardiac innervation, cardiopulmonary baroreceptor dysregulation, and arterial baroreceptor dysfunction.
content.RAAS system: Regulates blood pressure and is activated in hypertension and obesity, affecting vascular smooth muscle and fibroblasts.
content.systemic inflammation: First documented with AF post-cardiac surgery, and associated with nonsurgical AF.
content.persistence of AF: Reflects the substrate for AF; influenced by tissue characteristics.
content.role of ANS: Significant in triggering and sustaining AF.

genes.context: NLRP3 knockdown prevented AF inducibility in a mouse model, implicating the NLRP3 inflammasome in AF pathophysiology. NLRP3-blocking drugs are in development.
Activation of calcium-dependent calmodulin kinase II and jun kinase promotes ryanodine receptor dysfunction leading to spontaneous calcium release.

content.ANS Triggers AF: The autonomic nervous system (ANS) is a trigger for atrial fibrillation (AF) through several mechanisms including sympathetic and parasympathetic stimulation. Sympathetic stimulation increases inward current and delayed afterdepolarization, while parasympathetic stimulation shortens atrial effective refractory period, potentially triggering AF.
content.ANS Maintains AF: AF is maintained through sympathetic and parasympathetic hyperinnervation, spatial heterogeneity, and altered atrial electrophysiology, contributing to substrate. These changes lead to autonomic afferent reflex deficiencies and increase sympathetic activity.
content.Genetics: Both common forms and familial AF are heritable. Genetic studies have identified specific loci and variants associated with AF, with genes such as TTN, MYH7, MYH6, LMNA, and KCNQ1 being commonly associated with inherited cardiomyopathy or arrhythmia syndromes. Variants are more prevalent at a younger age of AF onset.
content.Health Inequities in AF Management: There are documented inequities in AF care related to gender, race, ethnicity, and social determinants of health (SDOH). Recommendations emphasize equitably delivered guideline-directed stroke risk reduction therapies to improve quality of life and prevent adverse outcomes.

genes.context: A UK Biobank study identified TTN loss of function variants in 0.44% of participants, 14% of whom had AF.

content.Overview: The guideline discusses atrial fibrillation (AF) management, highlighting disparities in treatment outcomes for women and individuals from under-represented ethnic groups (UREGs). Emphasis is placed on shared decision-making (SDM) to guide stroke reduction therapy decisions.
Key Points: Individuals from UREGs with AF are more symptomatic and have different quality of life (QOL) outcomes compared to other groups.
Women are referred for ablation later and less often than men, affecting outcomes.
Patients with adverse social determinants of health (SDOH) benefit from rhythm control therapy.
Lower socioeconomic status correlates with lower adherence to oral anticoagulation and increased hospitalizations, stroke, heart failure, and death.
The use of evidence-based decision aids may improve patient engagement and decision quality.

Recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with AF, use evidence-based decision aids to guide stroke reduction therapy treatment decisions throughout the disease course.

content.Synopsis: The document highlights the need for standardized SDM approaches, which can aid in stroke reduction therapies and improve patient-reported measures. These approaches are particularly beneficial for disadvantaged populations.

content.Title: 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation

Risk Models.Description: There are over 20 risk prediction models for incident atrial fibrillation (AF) in the community.
Risk Models.Main Model: The most widely replicated risk model for newly diagnosed AF is CHARGE-AF.
Risk Models.CHARGE-AF: Cohorts for Heart and Aging Research in Genomic Epidemiology Model for atrial fibrillation.
Risk Models.C2HEST Model: Derived and validated in Asian cohorts.

Screening.Methods: Various protocols including electrocardiographic recordings, consumer-based devices, and continuous external monitors.

Study Findings.AI Model: Algorithm able to risk-stratify older adults at risk for stroke; associated with increased AF detection.
Study Findings.Mass Population Screening: With a smartphone app rarely detected a new diagnosis of AF.
Study Findings.General Suggestion: Risk stratification models and screening programs need improvement to enhance outcomes and cost-effectiveness.

Tables.Table 7: C2HEST Risk Score for Detecting Incident AF

Recommendations for Basic Clinical Evalu.1: In patients with newly diagnosed AF, a transthoracic echocardiogram is recommended to assess cardiac structure, laboratory testing to include a complete blood count, metabolic panel, and thyroid function. Additional targeted testing for other conditions associated with AF is advised to determine stroke and bleeding risk factors and other underlying conditions for further management.
Recommendations for Basic Clinical Evalu.2: For patients with newly diagnosed AF, routine protocolized testing for ischemia, acute coronary syndrome (ACS), and pulmonary embolism (PE) is not recommended unless other signs or symptoms suggest these disorders.

content.Synopsis: Initial clinical evaluation should confirm the diagnosis of AF and identify clinical factors relevant to management. Targeted history and physical examination should be performed. An ECG assesses electrical abnormalities. A transthoracic echocardiogram provides information on chamber size, thickness, function, and underlying pathologies. Additional specialized testing may be pursued based on initial evaluations. Laboratory testing can detect or rule out other medical conditions associated with AF.

genes.context: Laboratory testing can detect other medical conditions that are associated with AF and would impact therapeutic decision making, such as CKD5, liver dysfunction, and hyperthyroidism.

recommendations.text: Among individuals without a known history of AF, it is recommended that an initial AF diagnosis be made by a clinician using visual interpretation of the electrocardiographic signals, regardless of the type of rhythm or monitoring device.

In patients with an intracardiac rhythm device capable of a diagnosis of AF, such as from an atrial pacemaker lead, a diagnosis of AF should only be made after it is visually confirmed by reviewing intracardiac tracings to exclude signal artifacts and other arrhythmias.
For patients who have had a systemic thromboembolic event without a known history of AF and in whom maximum sensitivity to detect AF is sought, an implantable cardiac monitor is reasonable.

Among patients with a diagnosis of AF, it is reasonable to infer AF frequency, duration, and burden using automated algorithms available from electrocardiographic monitors, implantable cardiac monitors, and cardiac rhythm devices with an atrial lead, recognizing that periodic review can be required to exclude other arrhythmias.

Among patients with AF in whom cardiac monitoring is advised, it is reasonable to recommend use of a consumer-accessible electrocardiographic device that provides a high-quality tracing to detect recurrences.

synopsis: Monitoring options for AF include a standard 12-lead ECG, continuously recording or loop-recording electrocardiographic monitors, implantable cardiac monitors, and cardiac rhythm management devices with an atrial lead. Photoplethysmography has been used to infer AF from irregular pulse patterns.
Electrocardiographic monitors often deploy automated algorithms for AF detection but initial diagnosis should rely on a health care professional's examination of the electrocardiographic tracing.
RCTs have demonstrated that implantable cardiac monitors exhibit the highest sensitivity in detecting AF compared with external ambulatory monitors.
Automated algorithms to analyze electrocardiographic devices have been generally found to be sufficiently reliable to infer the frequency, duration, and burden of AF among those with an AF diagnosis.
recommendation_specific_supportive_text: Although automated algorithms in various devices are generally reliable, professional oversight of electrocardiographic tracings remains necessary due to the imperfect test characteristics.
Electrocardiographic algorithms utilizing photoplethysmography may not be sufficient to reliably establish an AF diagnosis.
Cardiac rhythm devices with an atrial lead have been shown to detect AF validated against conventional surface electrocardiographic tracings.

content.Title: 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
content.Summary: This guideline discusses the management and prevention of atrial fibrillation (AF), focusing on lifestyle and risk factor modification. It highlights the importance of comprehensive guideline-directed lifestyle changes for patients at increased risk of AF, targeting obesity, physical inactivity, unhealthy alcohol consumption, smoking, diabetes, and hypertension.

Recommendations.Recommendation: Patients at increased risk of AF should receive comprehensive guideline-directed LRFM for AF.

Key Points: Randomized trials show that implantable cardiac monitors are most sensitive in detecting AF.
Manual review of all electrocardiographic strips may not be feasible, but automated algorithms generally suffice.
Cardiac monitoring is advised for detecting recurrences and screening among AF patients.
Lifestyle adjustments can reduce AF risk, including maintaining ideal weight, physical activity, and avoiding smoking or excessive alcohol.
Risk Factors: Obesity and physical inactivity increase the risk of newly diagnosed AF.
High-volume endurance training should be approached with caution.
Alcohol consumption enhances the risk of AF in a linear fashion.
Smoking is associated with increased AF risk.
Hypertension is a significant risk factor for AF.

content.Title: Secondary Prevention: Management of Comorbidities and Risk Factors

Sections.Title: Weight Loss in Individuals Who Are Overweight or Obese

Recommendation.Text: In patients with AF who are overweight or obese (with BMI >27 kg/m²), weight loss is recommended, with an ideal target of at least 10% weight loss to reduce AF symptoms, burden, recurrence, and progression to persistent AF.

Sections.Synopsis: Obesity contributes to the development and progression of AF by altering the atrial myocardium. Weight management is crucial in AF prevention. A BMI increase is linked with higher risk of postoperative and postablation AF.
Sections.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: RCTs show that weight loss in overweight and obese individuals with AF reduces arrhythmia symptoms, recurrence, and burden. Bariatric surgery improves sinus rhythm maintenance and reverses AF type.

Sections.Title: Physical Fitness

Recommendation.Text: In individuals with AF, moderate-to-vigorous exercise training to a target of 210 minutes per week is recommended to reduce AF symptoms and burden, increase maintenance of sinus rhythm, increase functional capacity, and improve QOL.

Sections.Synopsis: Aerobic exercise interventions benefit patients with nonpermanent AF by lowering arrhythmia burden and enhancing QOL. The ACTIVE-AF study supports this by demonstrating greater freedom from arrhythmia and improved QOL.
Sections.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: Aerobic exercise in patients with nonpermanent AF can enhance the maintenance of sinus rhythm and reduce AF burden.

Exercise Training.Recommendation: Exercise training should be moderate- to vigorous-intensity aerobic exercise, with a target of 210 minutes per week.
Exercise Training.Purpose: To reduce the frequency and duration of AF episodes and improve cardiorespiratory fitness and symptom severity.
Exercise Training.Cautions: Caution should be applied to ensure adequate ventricular rate control during exercise training and absence of atrial myopathy related to excessive exercise training.
Exercise Training.Limitations: There is little evidence that high-intensity aerobic exercise may be favorable over moderate-intensity activities.
Exercise Training.Associated Risks: Extreme exercise levels have been associated with higher incidence of AF.

Smoking Cessation.Recommendation: Patients with a history of AF who smoke cigarettes should be strongly advised to quit smoking and should receive GDMT for tobacco cessation.
Smoking Cessation.Justification: Smoking is associated with worse cardiovascular outcomes and death in AF patients.
Smoking Cessation.Associated Risks: Smoking was associated with increased risk for AF recurrence, stroke, heart failure, hospitalization, and death.

Alcohol Consumption.Recommendation: Patients with AF seeking a rhythm-control strategy should minimize or eliminate alcohol consumption.
Alcohol Consumption.Justification: Alcohol consumption was associated with a higher risk of AF episode after objectively confirmed alcohol consumption.

Caffeine Consumption.Recommendation: For patients with AF, recommending caffeine abstention to prevent AF episodes is of no benefit, although it may reduce symptoms in patients who report caffeine triggers or worsens AF symptoms.
Caffeine Consumption.Synopsis: Studies show caffeine within normal limits may not increase AF risk. Excessive consumption can trigger AF symptoms in some.
Caffeine Consumption.Supportive Text: N-of-1 trials and observational studies generally found no difference in AF episodes with caffeine consumption. Some studies indicate no increased AF risk with caffeine consumption. Caffeine metabolism-related gene studies found no protective effect of genetic variations on AF risk. Reports of caffeine triggering AF exist, though not objectively proven.

Diet and Dietary Supplementation.Overview: Promoting a healthy diet prevents cardiovascular disease. Evidence on dietary supplements for AF prevention is inconsistent due to misclassification and confounding factors.
Diet and Dietary Supplementation.Specific Nutrients: Omega-3 fatty acids show an inverse relationship with AF. Vitamin D isn't widely beneficial postoperatively but helps in deficient individuals. Ascorbic acid has shown mixed results in postoperative AF management.
Diet and Dietary Supplementation.Weight Management: Important for reducing AF symptoms, leading to various dietary recommendations. Low-carb diets may increase AF risk.

Caffeine Consumption.Diabetes: AF and diabetes increase cardiovascular mortality and death risk. Optimal glycemic control may lessen AF recurrence post-ablation in patients with both conditions.

Treatment of Hypertension.Recommendation: For patients with AF and hypertension, optimal BP control is recommended to reduce AF recurrence and AF-related cardiovascular events.
Treatment of Hypertension.Synopsis: Renal denervation and mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (MRAs) have been associated with decreased AF burden. Clinical trials of integrated comprehensive rhythm control therapies have shown longer arrhythmia-free survival and decreased AF burden. Specific programs targeting BP have consistently shown reduced AF incidence.
Treatment of Hypertension.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: In patients with AF, treatment of hypertension should aim for current BP guidelines to reduce stroke, bleeding, and other adverse outcomes.

Sleep.Recommendation: Among patients with AF, it may be reasonable to screen for obstructive sleep apnea, given its high prevalence, though the role of treating sleep-disordered breathing to maintain sinus rhythm is uncertain.
Sleep.Synopsis: Sleep Disordered Breathing (SDB) is a risk factor for AF. Treatment of SDB may reduce the risk of AF recurrence and AF burden, but RCTs have not been powered to establish a definitive connection.
Sleep.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: The prevalence of SDB is high in AF patients and often undetected. Formal study may establish a diagnosis of SDB.

content.Clinical Context: The document provides guidelines for the diagnosis and management of Atrial Fibrillation (AF). It highlights that patients with AF often have multiple conditions that can increase AF risk or are exacerbated by it. The guideline emphasizes comprehensive care approaches tailored to individual patients to improve outcomes and reduce AF burden.

Recommendations.Recommendation: Patients with AF should receive comprehensive care addressing guideline-directed lifestyle risk factor management (LRFM), AF symptoms, risk of stroke, and other associated medical conditions to reduce AF burden, progression, or consequences.

In patients with AF, use of clinical care pathways, such as nurse-led AF clinics, is reasonable to promote comprehensive, team-based care and to enhance adherence to evidence-based therapies for AF and associated conditions.

content.Synopsis: Randomized trials have shown that interventions aimed at AF risk factors can reduce AF burden. Clinical care pathways and algorithms, including LRFM, have been promising in trials. However, it is unclear if a multidisciplinary team leads to better outcomes. Coordination of care among clinicians is emphasized. Several studies and trials, such as RACE 3, have shown varied efficacy in AF management strategies.

risk_stratification.overview: Patients with atrial fibrillation (AF) have an increased risk of stroke with varying intensity. Risk scores based on clinical factors are used to evaluate this risk.

recommendations.recommendation: Patients with AF should be evaluated for their annual risk of thromboembolic events using a validated clinical risk score, such as CHA2DS2-VASc.

Patients with AF should be evaluated for factors indicating a higher risk of bleeding, such as previous bleeding and drug use that increases bleeding risk, to identify interventions to prevent bleeding on anticoagulation.
Patients with AF at intermediate annual risk of thromboembolic events can benefit from considering factors that might modify their risk of stroke to help inform decision-making.
For patients deemed at high risk for stroke, bleeding risk scores should not be used in isolation to determine eligibility for oral anticoagulation.

risk_stratification.additional_notes: Factors such as age, hypertension, prior bleeding history, and clinical conditions play significant roles in risk assessment and management decisions.

content.conclusion: Assessment of stroke risk should be calibrated against actual stroke rates, and newer risk scores consider more factors. Risk discrimination is improved using predictors like CHA2DS2-VASc score.

content.figure: Rates of Stroke by Stroke Risk Score Levels in Different Cohorts
content.description: The graph depicts the overall stroke rate in atrial fibrillation cohorts sorted by descending stroke rates (events per 100 person-years). The CHA2DS2-VASc score is considered the most validated for stroke prediction, but despite extensive use, it has shown suboptimal performance in certain populations, prompting the development of new scores like ATRIA and GARFIELD-AF. These newer scores provide modest improvements in risk discrimination while also including additional variables such as smoking status, renal disease, and dementia.
clinical_notes: The CHA2DS2-VASc score is the preferred validated measure for stroke risk in atrial fibrillation.
Newer risk scores like ATRIA and GARFIELD-AF include additional parameters for enhanced risk prediction.
The GARFIELD-AF score incorporates mortality and bleeding risk to aid comprehensive patient discussions.
Decisions regarding stroke prevention therapy in AF patients must weigh the risks of stroke, bleeding, treatment benefit, and patient preferences.

Scores.Potential Advantages: CHADS2 was superior to existing risk classification schemes

Text Analysis: These scores are problematic to use in clinical decision-making because they incorporate several clinical factors that increase the risks of both stroke and bleeding such as age, hypertension, renal disease, and previous stroke.

Assessment of risk factors specific for bleeding may suggest interventions to reduce bleeding risk, such as discontinuing antiplatelet medications or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory medications or the use of LAAO devices.
The decision to treat with OACs for stroke prevention in patients with a CHA2DS2-VASc of 1 may require additional discussion with patients.

content.Risk Factor Definitions: The image provides definitions for the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score in atrial fibrillation, detailing conditions such as heart failure, hypertension, age, diabetes, vascular disease, and sex category as key factors in calculating stroke risk.
content.Additional Risk Factors: Additional risk factors for stroke not included in CHA₂DS₂-VASc include factors like higher AF burden/long duration, persistent/permanent AF versus paroxysmal, obesity, HCM, poorly controlled hypertension, low eGFR, proteinuria, and enlarged LA volume.
content.Thromboembolic Event Rates: The table provides thromboembolic event rates by point score for ATRIA, CHA₂DS₂, and CHA₂DS₂-VASc scores, showing increased risk with higher scores.

content.Summary: The guideline discusses risk-based selection of oral anticoagulation for stroke prevention in patients with atrial fibrillation (AF). The recommendations focus on personalized decision-making based on the individual risks of stroke and bleeding. The consideration of anticoagulation therapy should be guided by the patient's risk factors and regular reevaluation is advised.
Key Recommendations: For patients diagnosed with AF with an estimated annual risk of stroke >2%, therapy should aim to reduce stroke risk based on individual thromboembolism risk.
Periodic reevaluation of stroke risk and therapy choice is recommended for AF patients to reassess risks and benefits.
Important Notes: A bleeding risk score should not be used in isolation.
Stroke prevention therapy decisions should consider both benefits and harms of anticoagulation.

content.General Information: The guideline discusses oral anticoagulants for atrial fibrillation (AF) management. Vitamin K antagonists, like warfarin, have been used since the 1950s but have limitations, including a narrow therapeutic window and frequent monitoring requirements. Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) are now recommended as first-line therapy over warfarin for most patients with AF. Warfarin is still used in some cases due to cost considerations. The DOACs discussed include apixaban, dabigatran, edoxaban, and rivaroxaban, showing a lower risk of major bleeding compared to warfarin. Antithrombotic therapy recommendations are provided based on thromboembolic risk assessments.

genes.context: The use of warfarin involves narrow therapeutic window based on international normalized ratios (INRs), frequent monitoring, more frequent drug interactions (mainly through CYP2C9), dietary restrictions, and low clinical safety profile.

content.Anticoagulation: Has been shown to be superior to antiplatelet therapy to reduce stroke risk.
content.DOACs vs Warfarin: DOACs favored for lower risk of stroke, systemic embolism, and ICH. Meta-analysis showed consistent benefit of DOACs in broad range of patients.
content.DOAC Studies: Randomized trials show DOACs superior for reduction of stroke risk with lower CHADS2 scores.
content.Aspirin Use: Aspirin used as antiplatelet therapy, not shown superior to apixaban for stroke prevention.
content.Markov Decision Model: Anticoagulation for stroke rate of 1.7%, and systemic embolism risk threshold of 2%.
content.Valvular Heart Disease (VHD): Recommendations for selection of therapy in VHD.
content.VHD and Valve Repair: Bioprosthetic valves excluded from DOAC trials.

clinical_guidelines.title: Antithrombotic Options in Patients With Atrial Fibrillation (AF)

anticoagulation_choices.exceptions: Except for mitral stenosis or mechanical heart valve

anticoagulation_choices.not_recommended: Aspirin either alone or with clopidogrel
anticoagulation_choices.reason: Not recommended to reduce stroke risk

anticoagulation_choices.additional_note: If no risk factors for stroke, aspirin to prevent thromboembolic events not recommended

discussion.aspirin_comparison: Aspirin therapy compared with no treatment showed a marginally higher risk of major bleeding and did not decrease the rate of cardiomebolic stroke.

discussion.meta_analysis: A meta-analysis of 7 trials showed aspirin reduced stroke by 19%, though not statistically significant.

content.Title: Considerations in Managing Anticoagulants
content.Source: 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
content.Synopsis: This guideline focuses on selecting optimal oral anticoagulants (OAC) for patients with atrial fibrillation (AF), emphasizing factors such as efficacy, safety, insurance coverage, renal/hepatic function, drug interactions, and patient preferences. Recommendations include monitoring drug interactions, particularly with direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) affected by inducers or inhibitors like CYP3A4 and p-glycoprotein. The importance of maintaining therapeutic drug levels and ensuring consistency in dietary habits, such as vitamin K intake, is highlighted to minimize preventable thromboembolism or bleeding and improve survival rates.

Key Recommendations.Recommendation: For patients with AF receiving DOACs, optimize management of drug interactions, especially with CYP3A4 and/or p-glycoprotein inhibitors or inducers.
For patients with AF receiving warfarin, target INR between 2 and 3. Ensure optimal drug interaction management, consistent vitamin K intake, and routine monitoring to minimize bleeding risks.
Avoid nonevidence-based doses of DOACs in patients with AF to minimize thromboembolism and bleeding risks.

Additional Notes.Recommendations: Referenced studies supporting the recommendations are summarized in an Online Data Supplement.
Additional Notes.Drug Specifics: Discusses contraindications and dose adjustments linked to drug interactions, especially with rivaroxaban and apixaban.

Associated Conditions: Drug interaction risk in patients on DOACs
genes.context: Strong CYP3A4 inhibitors such as ketoconazole, itraconazole, and ritonavir may significantly increase DOAC plasma levels and the risk of bleeding.

content.Title: OACs Pharmacokinetic Characteristics and Dosing

Metabolism.Warfarin: S-isomer: CYP2C9, R-isomer: CYP1A2, CYP2C19, CYP3A4

Excretion.Warfarin: 0% renal, very little warfarin excreted unchanged in urine

Renal dosing adjustment based on actual.Rivaroxaban: CrCl >50 mL/min, 20 mg daily with the biggest meal

Renal dosing adjustment for CrCl 15-30 m.Rivaroxaban: CrCl 15-50, 15 mg daily with the biggest meal
Renal dosing adjustment for CrCl 15-30 m.Apixaban: 2.5 mg twice daily under specific conditions

CYP3A4 inhibitors.Warfarin: Adjust dose based on INR trends
CYP3A4 inhibitors.Rivaroxaban: Avoid with combined P-glycoprotein and strong CYP3A4 inhibitors
CYP3A4 inhibitors.Apixaban: Reduced dose or avoid when combined with inhibitors

P-glycoprotein/CYP3A4 inducers.Warfarin: Adjust dose based on INR trends

genes.context: Warfarin metabolism S-isomer: CYP2C9
Warfarin metabolism R-isomer: CYP1A2
Warfarin metabolism R-isomer: CYP2C19
Warfarin metabolism R-isomer: CYP3A4; Rivaroxaban: CYP3A4/5; Apixaban: CYP3A4
Rivaroxaban metabolism: CYP3A4/5

content.Title: DOAC Laboratory Monitoring
content.Description: Guideline for monitoring direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) based on severity of disease and monitoring intervals.

Notes.HAS-BLED Score: Includes factors like uncontrolled hypertension, abnormal renal or hepatic function, history of stroke, among others.
Notes.Child-Pugh Scoring: Used to grade severity of liver disease.

recommendation.Description: In patients with stroke or TIA of undetermined cause, initial cardiac monitoring and, if needed, extended monitoring with an implantable loop recorder are reasonable to improve detection of AF.

content.synopsis: Approximately 25% to 30% of ischemic strokes remain cryptogenic after standard stroke evaluation. A large proportion of these events are presumed to be related to occult AF, particularly those of embolic appearance. Antiplatelet therapy (APT) is the recommended treatment of choice for patients with cryptogenic stroke, including embolic stroke of undetermined source. In patients with AF in general, however, anticoagulation has been shown to be superior to APT for stroke risk reduction. Detection of occult AF after stroke may have significant therapeutic implications. Randomized trials have shown longer durations of cardiac monitoring result in higher rates of AF detection after stroke. Limited data exist on the effects of extended monitoring on risk reduction of recurrent stroke or poststroke mortality.
content.recommendation_specific_support: Growing evidence supports the use of extended cardiac monitoring for the identification of occult AF in patients with cryptogenic stroke. The EMBRACE trial and the CRYSTAL-AF trial have shown that extended monitoring was associated with higher rates of AF detection.

content.guideline: 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation

recommendations.recommendation: For patients with a device-detected atrial high-rate episode (AHRE) lasting ≥24 hours and with a CHA2DS2-VASc score ≥2 or equivalent stroke risk, it is reasonable to initiate oral anticoagulation within a SDM framework that considers episode duration and individual patient risk.

For patients with a device-detected AHRE lasting between 5 minutes and 24 hours and with a CHA2DS2-VASc score ≥3 or equivalent stroke risk, it may be reasonable to initiate anticoagulation within a SDM framework that considers episode duration and individual patient risk.
Patients with a device-detected AHRE lasting ≤5 minutes and without another indication for oral anticoagulation should not receive oral anticoagulation.

key_points: AHREs detected by a cardiovascular implantable electronic device are associated with a stroke risk lower than that of clinical AF that varies according to the episode duration and CHA2DS2-VASc score.
Episodes lasting ≥24 hours are associated with a significant risk of stroke or systemic embolism that may be reduced with oral anticoagulation.
Short episodes, commonly defined as ≤5 minutes, are not associated with clinical events.
There is an interaction between AHRE duration and CHA2DS2-VASc score, suggesting both may be used to guide oral anticoagulation candidacy.

content.title: Consideration of Oral Anticoagulation for Device-Detected AHREs According to Patient Stroke Risk by CHA2DS2-VASc Score and Episode Duration
discussion: The guideline discusses management strategies for patients with subclinical atrial fibrillation (SCAF) and device-detected atrial high-rate episodes (AHREs) based on the patient's stroke risk, as measured by the CHA2DS2-VASc score, and the AHRE duration.

Three categories are defined based on patient risk and AHRE duration: low risk (Circle A) - observe for atrial fibrillation (AF) development; intermediate risk (Circle B) - periodic assessment with a focus on other OAC indications and changes in CHA2DS2-VASc score; high risk (Circle C) - anticoagulation indicated if atrial fibrillation is confirmed via ECG or AF certainty is high.
For intermediate-risk patients, ongoing studies (ARTESiA and NOAH trials) are providing additional evidence to guide management.
Circles B and C patients, at intermediate to high risk, may require anticoagulation depending on the length and burden of AHREs.
The guideline elaborates on the lack of necessity for anticoagulation in low-risk patients, while high-risk patients might benefit from tailored anticoagulation strategies.
The CHA2DS2-VASc score adjustment based on sex is noted, with different thresholds for men and women.

content.Summary: Although oral anticoagulation is standard to reduce ischemic stroke risk in AF patients, it can be contraindicated for some due to bleeding risk. Surgical and percutaneous techniques like LAAO (Left Atrial Appendage Occlusion) are alternatives. These can reduce ischemic stroke risk, obviating the need for long-term anticoagulation in select patients, especially those undergoing cardiac surgery.

Recommendations.2a B-NR: For AF patients with a moderate to high stroke risk and a nonreversible anticoagulation contraindication, percutaneous LAAO is reasonable.
Recommendations.2b B-R: For AF patients with high stroke and bleeding risk on anticoagulation, percutaneous LAAO might be an alternative to oral anticoagulation, considering risk and procedural knowledge.

content.Synopsis: Evidence supports OACs reduce ischemic stroke risk in AF patients but are sometimes unsuitable. Studies show percutaneous LAAO like the Watchman device can be effective, with a lower stroke risk compared to OAC with warfarin.
Studies: A prospective nonrandomized study showed decreased ischemic stroke risk with a durable pLAAO implant.
Feasibility Study With Watchman Left Atrial Appendage Closure Technology showed lower ischemic stroke in patients with the Watchman (2.3%) vs. expected scores (7.3%).
The EWOLUTION registry showed high success (98.5%) and low ischemic stroke (1.1%) with Watchman.
The PINNACLE FLX registry indicated high procedural success (98.8%) and low safety endpoint occurrence (0.5%).
content.Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text: pLAAO has been evaluated in AF patients contraindicated to OAC, with substantial variation in antithrombotic regimens.

content.clinical_guideline: The guideline focuses on the evaluation of long-term anticoagulation therapy in patients with atrial fibrillation (AF) and the use of the Watchman Device for reducing the risk of stroke. It addresses situations where long-term anticoagulation is contraindicated or reasonable, reviews studies on surgical left atrial appendage occlusion (S-LAAO), and discusses recommendations for patients undergoing cardiac surgery with a CHA₂DS₂-VASc score ≥2.

clinical_studies.name: ASAP-TOO
clinical_studies.focus: Safety and efficacy of LAAO in AF patients ineligible for oral anticoagulation.

clinical_studies.name: PROTECT AF
clinical_studies.focus: Comparing pLAAO with the Watchman device versus warfarin.

clinical_studies.name: LAAOS III
clinical_studies.focus: S-LAAO in AF patients undergoing CABG/valve surgery.

content.recommendations: Recommendations for LAA exclusion/excision in patients with AF undergoing cardiac surgery based on CHA₂DS₂-VASc score.

Main Points: LAOOS III study included 4,770 patients with atrial fibrillation (AF) and CHA2DS2-VASc score ≥2 undergoing cardiac surgery, randomized to S-LAAO or no S-LAAO.
Transesophageal echocardiogram (TEE) was recommended to assess successful closure.
Primary endpoint of stroke or systemic embolism occurred in 4.8% of S-LAAO group and 7.0% of no S-LAAO group.
S-LAAO provided additional benefit to oral anticoagulation without increasing risk of adverse events.
Meta-analysis of 5 RCTs and 22 observational studies showed significant decrease in stroke and thromboembolism with S-LAAO.
Substantial heterogeneity in S-LAAO methods exists.
Incomplete S-LAAO is associated with increased risk of stroke and thromboembolism.
S-LAAO reduced stroke and thromboembolism by 33% in addition to oral anticoagulation.
Techniques: Amputation and closure (used in 56% of cases)
Statistics: >80% of subjects in both groups were receiving oral anticoagulation.
Systematic review found no statistically significant difference in stroke rates between S-LAAO/no S-LAAO subjects on oral anticoagulation vs. off oral anticoagulation.
LAACS study with 187 subjects showed reduction in stroke risk in S-LAAO group.

content.Title: 6.6. Active Bleeding on Anticoagulant Therapy and Reversal Drugs

Recommendations.Details: In patients with AF receiving dabigatran who develop life-threatening bleeding, treatment with idarucizumab is recommended to rapidly reverse dabigatran’s anticoagulation effect.

In patients with AF receiving dabigatran who develop life-threatening bleeding, treatment with activated prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC) is reasonable to reverse dabigatran’s anticoagulation effect if idarucizumab is unavailable.

In patients with AF receiving factor Xa inhibitors who develop life-threatening bleeding, treatment with either andexanet alfa (apixaban or rivaroxaban,* edoxaban‡) or 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate† is recommended to rapidly reverse factor Xa inhibitor’s anticoagulation effect.

In patients with AF receiving warfarin who develop life-threatening bleeding, treatment with 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (if available) in addition to intravenous vitamin K is recommended to rapidly achieve INR correction over fresh frozen plasma and intravenous vitamin K treatment.

In patients with AF who develop major gastrointestinal bleeding, resumption of oral anticoagulation therapy may be reasonable after correction of reversible causes of bleeding and reassessment of its long-term benefits and risks.

content.Synopsis: About 2% to 4% of patients who receive OACs experience major bleeding. Strategies include use of idarucizumab for dabigatran and andexanet alfa for Factor Xa inhibitors. Activated charcoal and hemodialysis may assist in drug elimination.

genes.context: In patients with AF receiving warfarin who develop life-threatening bleeding, treatment with 4-factor prothrombin complex concentrate (if available) in addition to intravenous vitamin K is recommended to rapidly achieve INR correction over fresh frozen plasma and intravenous vitamin K treatment.

Idarucizumab.Dosing: 5 g (2 separate vials of 2.5 g/vial) intravenous infusion over 5 min
Idarucizumab.Monitoring: Coagulation parameters (aPTT, diluted thrombin time, or ecarin clotting time) between 12 and 24 h
Others: No procoagulant effect based on endogenous thrombin potential

Andexanet alfa.Class: A recombinant modified human factor Xa protein
Andexanet alfa.Monitoring: Current commercial anti-Xa activity assays are unsuitable
Others: No FDA indication for other factor Xa inhibitors

4-Factor PCC.Class: PCC: coagulation factors II, VII, IX, and X
FDA Indications: Major bleeding or need for urgent surgery
Invasive procedure in patients receiving VKAs
4-Factor PCC.Dosing: Warfarin reversal based on pretreatment INR
4-Factor PCC.Monitoring: Warfarin reversal: Repeat INR within 30 min
Others: May not be indicated for patients with thromboembolic events

Activated PCC.Class: Nonactivated factors II, IX, and X
FDA Indications: Control and prevention of bleeding episodes, perioperative management
Prophylaxis to prevent or reduce bleeding frequency in patients with hemophilia A and B
Others: Not effective to reverse factor Xa inhibitors

content.title: Bleeding Events in DOAC Pivotal Clinical Trials
content.overview: The table compares bleeding events associated with various oral anticoagulants in different clinical trials, namely RE-LY, ARISTOTLE, ENGAGE AF-TIMI 48, and ROCKET AF.
key findings: Dabigatran has different incidence rates of major, gastrointestinal, and intracranial bleeding when compared to Warfarin across various trials.
Apixaban generally shows lower rates of bleeding events compared to Warfarin.
Edoxaban presents varied results across different bleeding categories when compared to Warfarin.
Rivaroxaban exhibits higher major bleeding rates compared to Warfarin in one trial and mixed results in others.

measures: May hold an anticoagulant until hemostasis is achieved

options: Andexanet alfa for apixaban or rivaroxaban
Activated PCC if idarucizumab is not available
4-factor PCC and intravenous vitamin K for warfarin

content.section: 6.6.1. Management of Patients With AF and ICH

recommendations.recommendation: In patients with AF and conditions associated with very high risk of thromboembolic events (>5%/year), such as rheumatic heart disease or a mechanical heart valve, early (1-2 weeks) resumption of anticoagulation after ICH is reasonable to reduce the risk of thromboembolic events.

In patients with AF and ICH, delayed (4-8 weeks) resumption of anticoagulation may be considered to balance the risks of thromboembolic and hemorrhagic complications after careful risk benefit assessment.

In patients with AF and conditions associated with high risk of recurrent ICH (eg, cerebral amyloid angiopathy) anticoagulation-sparing strategies (eg, LAAO) may be considered to reduce the risk of recurrent hemorrhage.

content.synopsis: ICH refers to bleeding within compartments of the cranial vault, and management considers mechanism and most broadly traumatic vs nontraumatic etiologies.

content.Forms of Intracranial Hemorrhage (ICH): The image describes different forms of ICH classified by mechanism, which includes traumatic (e.g., subarachnoid hemorrhage, intraparenchymal hemorrhage, etc.) and nontraumatic/spontaneous (e.g., aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage, intraparenchymal hemorrhage due to cerebral amyloid angiopathy, etc.).
content.Clinical Decision-Making: The guideline highlights the risk of recurrence in patients with hypertensive parenchymal hemorrhage, hemorrhagic transformation after ischemic stroke, cerebral amyloid angiopathy-associated hemorrhage, chronic subdural hemorrhage, and the need for individual risk-benefit assessments.
Recommendations: Rheumatic valvular disease has an associated >5% per year risk of embolic events.
Valvular prosthetic risk varies with the type and position of the valve.
ICH is associated with a high risk of thromboembolic and recurrent hemorrhage.
Limited data exist on the safe resumption of anticoagulation post-ICH.

content.Title: 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
Key Points: Anticoagulation is resumed at 7 to 8 weeks after intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH).
Oral anticoagulation (OAC) may reduce the risk of ischemic stroke and systemic embolism but can increase the risk of recurrent ICH.
For patients with atrial fibrillation (AF) at high risk of thromboembolism, anticoagulation is recommended, but may be prohibitive in some due to recurrent ICH risk.
Cerebral amyloid angiopathy is a common cause of lobar hemorrhage in older adults.
Left atrial appendage (LAA) closure can be considered for those not eligible for anticoagulation.
Recommendations are provided for periprocedural management of anticoagulation in patients with AF, including those on warfarin and direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs).
Temporary cessation of anticoagulation without bridging is recommended in certain cases.
Maintaining anticoagulant parameters as close to normal as possible is emphasized.

recommendations: To mitigate thromboembolic risk during interruption of warfarin for a procedure or surgery, bridging anticoagulation can be administered.
Interruption of warfarin without bridging anticoagulation was supported in several studies.
Observational studies show increased bleeding risk without a difference in thromboembolic risk with bridging.
DOAC (Direct Oral Anticoagulants) interruptions are generally considered safe for high bleeding risk surgeries.
Two RCTs and observational studies showed little to no difference in the risk of pocket hematoma.
factors: Patient-specific factors for bleeding include abnormal liver and kidney function, bleeding or clotting disorders, and use of antiplatelet or nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs.
procedures: High bleeding risk procedures include intra-abdominal, pelvic, orthopedic, neurosurgical, cardiac, and transvenous lead extraction surgeries.
Low bleeding risk procedures include certain dental, endoscopic, and ophthalmologic procedures.
study_data: The BRIDGE study included patients with a mean CHADS2 score of 2.3.
The PAUSE study included patients with a mean CHADS2 score of 2.1 and CHA2DS2-VASc score of 3.5.
A smaller RCT showed similar outcomes to BRIDGE suggesting no need for bridging in DOACs.

content.Management: The guideline outlines a flowchart for managing periprocedural anticoagulation in patients with atrial fibrillation undergoing surgery or invasive procedures. It provides different pathways for patients based on their anticoagulation needs, procedure types, and risk assessments.
content.Procedures: Categorizes procedures based on bleeding risk (low, high) and guides anticoagulation management accordingly.
content.Anticoagulation: Recommends temporary cessation of oral anticoagulation without bridging for most procedures unless the patient has a recent stroke, transient ischemic attack, or a mechanical valve.
content.Risk Assessment: Utilizes CHA₂DS₂-VASc scores and risk of thromboembolism to guide the management of anticoagulation.

guidelines.title: Anticoagulation in Specific Populations

recommendations.details: In patients with AF and an increased risk for stroke who undergo PCI, DOACs are preferred over VKAs in combination with APT to reduce the risk of clinically relevant bleeding.

In most patients with AF who take oral anticoagulation and undergo PCI, early discontinuation of aspirin (1-4 wk) and continuation of dual antithrombotic therapy with OAC and a P2Y12 inhibitor is preferred over triple therapy (OAC, P2Y12 inhibitor, and aspirin) to reduce the risk of clinically relevant bleeding.

guidelines.synopsis: Encountering a patient with AF at an increased risk for stroke who undergoes PCI for stable CAD or ACS is a common clinical scenario. The addition of APT to OAC after PCI introduces a risk for clinically significant bleeding. RCTs have compared the safety and efficacy of antithrombotic regimens after PCI is performed in patients with AF. It is consistently shown that DOACs, when used in conjunction with APT, are associated with a lower risk of clinically relevant bleeding compared to VKAs.

trials.name: BRIDGE trial
trials.summary: Randomized 1,884 patients with AF or AFL to bridging anticoagulation before and after the procedure with dalteparin versus placebo. No bridging was superior to bridging with respect to major bleeding and was noninferior with respect to thromboembolism.

clinical_studies.findings: Trial showed that dual antithrombotic therapy with Dabigatran versus Triple Therapy with Warfarin showed a relative reduction in major bleeding events.
Showed a 42% relative risk reduction for major bleeding compared with VKA-based regimens.
Showed edoxaban-based regimens were noninferior to VKA-based regimens in terms of clinically relevant bleeding after PCI.
The study compared outcomes in antiplatelet and anticoagulant therapy in patients with Dual Antiplatelet Therapy post PCI.
Showed substantially lower major bleeding incidence with dabigatran in comparison to other dual therapies.

clinical_recommendations.context: For patients with AF and CCD beyond 1 year after revascularization, without history of stent thrombosis, anticoagulation monotherapy is recommended over combination therapy.

meta_analysis.findings: A meta-analysis of PIONEER AF-PCI, RE-DUAL, and AUGUSTUS demonstrated a 40% relative risk reduction in major bleeding. However, an increased risk of stent thrombosis was associated with DAT compared with triple therapy.

bleeding_risks.context: DAT with DOAC and P2Y12 inhibitor showed increased risks of major bleeding.
bleeding_risks.note: Such findings indicate the importance of extended triple therapy for different perceived risks.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.RCT Comparison: An RCT compared rivaroxaban monotherapy with a combination therapy of rivaroxaban and a single antiplatelet therapy (APT) in patients with atrial fibrillation (AF) and coronary artery disease (CAD) not requiring revascularization. Rivaroxaban monotherapy was noninferior for the primary outcome of stroke, systemic embolism, myocardial infarction (MI), unstable angina requiring revascularization, or death from any cause. It was superior for major bleeding. However, another trial showed rivaroxaban monotherapy did not prove noninferior for stroke or systemic embolism.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.PAD Recommendation: In patients with AF and concomitant stable peripheral artery disease (PAD), monotherapy oral anticoagulation is reasonable over dual therapy to reduce the risk of bleeding.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.WAVE Trial: In the WAVE (Warfarin Antiplatelet Vascular Evaluation) trial, dual therapy did not reduce mortality or major cardiovascular events and increased life-threatening bleeding risk compared to monotherapy.

content.title: 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation

recommendations.recommendation: For patients with AF at elevated risk for stroke and CKD stage 3, treatment with warfarin or, preferably, evidence-based doses of direct thrombin or factor Xa inhibitors is recommended to reduce the risk of stroke.
For patients with AF at elevated risk for stroke and CKD stage 4, treatment with warfarin or labeled doses of DOACs is reasonable to reduce the risk of stroke.

For patients with AF at elevated risk for stroke and who have end-stage CKD (CrCl <15 mL/min) or are on dialysis, it might be reasonable to prescribe warfarin (INR 2.0-3.0) or an evidence-based dose of apixaban for oral anticoagulation to reduce the risk of stroke.

synopsis: CKD is a risk factor for stroke in patients with AF.
The prevalence of AF among patients with renal failure on dialysis is high, including asymptomatic AF.
Conflicting data exist on whether AF is a risk factor for stroke in patients on dialysis.
VKA use is limited by a decreased therapeutic range in patients with severe CKD.
evidence: Aspirin reduced the risk of stroke in patients with similar kidney function.
In the AVERROES trial, apixaban showed similar efficacy to warfarin in patients with a GFR of 25 to 50 mL/min.
recommendation_specific_supportive_text: Early trials demonstrated the role of warfarin anticoagulation in preventing stroke but did not enroll or separately report outcomes in patients with CKD.
The SPAF III trial showed that dose-adjusted warfarin reduced the risk of stroke compared to aspirin and fixed low-dose warfarin.

content.Overview: The guideline discusses the management of atrial fibrillation (AF) in patients with valvular heart disease (VHD), highlighting recommendations for anticoagulant therapy.
Key Points: Long-term anticoagulation with warfarin is recommended for patients with rheumatic mitral stenosis or moderate to greater severity mitral valve disease and history of AF.
Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) are recommended over vitamin K antagonists (VKAs) for patients with AF and valve disease other than moderate to greater mitral stenosis or a mechanical heart valve.
Patients with AF and VHD have a higher prevalence of stroke and systemic thromboembolism.
Warfarin is the anticoagulant of choice for high-risk patients, particularly with significant mitral stenosis or mechanical heart valves.

Recommendations.Description: In patients with rheumatic mitral stenosis or mitral stenosis of moderate or greater severity and history of AF, long-term anticoagulation with warfarin is recommended over DOACs.
In patients with AF and valve disease other than moderate or greater mitral stenosis or a mechanical heart valve, DOACs are recommended over VKAs.

Clinical Trials: The ARISTOTLE trial and others provide valuable data on the effectiveness of DOACs compared to warfarin in patients with CrCl restrictions.
Historical data indicate high risks of stroke and thromboembolic events in patients with mitral stenosis and AF.

content.overview: This clinical guideline focuses on the management of atrial flutter (AFL), particularly after catheter ablation. It outlines recommendations for anticoagulation therapy based on patient history and risk of thromboembolic events. Patients with AFL who have undergone ablation may require anticoagulant therapy similar to those used for atrial fibrillation (AF), and those with high thrombembolic risk without a history of AF should be monitored closely.

recommendations.recommendation: For patients with AFL, anticoagulant therapy is recommended according to the same risk profile used for AF.
In patients with AFL who undergo successful cardioversion or ablation in restoration of sinus rhythm, anticoagulation should be continued for at least 4 weeks postprocedure.

Patients with typical AFL who have undergone successful CTI ablation and have had AF previously detected before AFL ablation should receive ongoing oral anticoagulation postablation as indicated for AF.

Patients with typical AFL who have undergone successful CTI ablation and are deemed to be at high thromboembolic risk, without any known previous history of AF, should receive follow-up and arrhythmia monitoring to detect silent AF if they are not receiving ongoing anticoagulation in view of significant risk.

Patients with typical AFL who have undergone successful CTI ablation without any known previous history of AF who are at high risk for development of AF should be considered for long-term anticoagulation if thromboembolic risk assessment suggests high risk for stroke.

content.Description: This clinical guideline addresses anticoagulation for Typical (CTI-Dependent) Atrial Flutter (AFL). It outlines different pathways based on the history of atrial fibrillation (AF) and the success of AFL ablation.
Key Points: AFL is associated with increased thromboembolic risk.
Oral anticoagulation benefits and risks are difficult to ascertain due to the coexistence of AFL and AF.
Patients with typical AFL and no history of AF have a higher stroke incidence compared to the general population.
Anticoagulation recommendations vary based on the history of AF and the thromboembolic risk profile.

content.summary: The guideline discusses atrial fibrillation (AF) management post-ablation, focusing on the incidence of AF recurrence, risk assessments, and long-term monitoring. It emphasizes continued anticoagulation due to thromboembolic risks post-ablation. Factors like left atrial enlargement and other conditions contribute to AF post-ablation. Rate control methods for AF are evaluated, discussing lenient vs. strict rate control in patients.
clinical_notes: Post-ablation AF incidence is influenced by patient's history of AF before ablation.
Oral anticoagulation reduces systemic thromboembolic risk in AF.
Patients with new-onset AF after AFL ablation require close follow-up.
Implantable cardiac monitors are useful post-ablation.
Specific risk factors for post-ablation AF include left atrial enlargement and inducible AF.
Rate control can be lenient or strict depending on the patient's condition.

content.Rate-control and rhythm-control strategi: Studies indicate comparable outcomes in patients with AF. Key studies include AFFIRM and HOT CAFE, showing similar results between strategies.
content.Meta-analysis findings: Rate-control strategies found to be effective in reducing all-cause mortality, major bleeding, and cardiac mortality. However, some studies suggest variability in outcomes.
content.Target resting heart rates and outcomes: RACE II study suggests target heart rates (<110 bpm) do not significantly influence outcomes.
content.Challenges in rate-control strategies: Limitations noted due to specific study populations and short follow-up periods. Data may not be applicable to patients with paroxysmal AF or HF.
content.Pharmacological agents for rate control: Focus is on calcium channel blockers and beta blockers. These are preferred due to positive impact on ventricular rate control.
content.Implications for HF patients: Patients with HF may benefit from specific rate-control drugs and strategies such as ICDs and cardiac resynchronization therapy.

recommendations_for_acute_rate_control.recommendation: Patients with AF and rapid ventricular response who are hemodynamically stable should use beta blockers or nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers.
For those where beta blockers or nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers are ineffective, digoxin may be used.
In cases of rapid ventricular response, adding intravenous magnesium may be beneficial.
Intravenous amiodarone is advisable for critical instability or decompensated heart failure.
Avoid nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers in severe left ventricular dysfunction.

content.Synopsis: Controlling the ventricular rate in atrial fibrillation (AF) with a rapid ventricular response in the acute setting can be challenging and is crucial for symptom control. Some agents may not be preferable due to underlying comorbidities. For patients needing intravenous rate-control agents, hypotension or decompensated heart failure may limit options.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.description: A placebo-controlled trial on continuous intravenous diltiazem infusion for heart rate control during AF and AFL showed better maintenance of response compared to placebo. Another RCT compared diltiazem, digoxin, or amiodarone, and found diltiazem reduced heart rate quickly and more effectively. Amiodarone showed faster control in the emergency setting.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.study_outcomes: Patients receiving diltiazem infusion showed a slower loss of response. The diltiazem group achieved rate control faster than digoxin or amiodarone groups.

Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.description: Intravenous digoxin was less effective than diltiazem in several RCTs. However, intravenous magnesium appears beneficial over conventional therapy, often showing superior control for rapid AF.
Recommendation-Specific Supportive Text.study_outcomes: Intravenous diltiazem achieved more rapid heart rate control than digoxin. Intravenous magnesium was superior in achieving rate control in various studies.

evidence_summary: Intravenous amiodarone shown to be effective in controlling ventricular rates in critically ill patients; retrospective studies reported significant heart rate decrease without BP decrease compared with IV diltiazem and digoxin.

One randomized study of critically ill AF patients compared diltiazem infusion, amiodarone bolus/infusion, and amiodarone regimen; diltiazem allowed better rate control over 24 hours but was associated with higher hypotension requiring discontinuation.

Limited data for non-dihydropyridine calcium-channel blockers (eg, diltiazem) in HFrEF due to presumed negative inotropic effects; retrospective analyses show increased morbidity (eg, acute kidney injury within 48 hours, increased HF worsening) in patients with LVEF ≤50% receiving diltiazem.

content.Title: 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation

Recommendations.Details: In patients with AF, beta blockers or nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (diltiazem, verapamil) are recommended for long-term rate control with the choice of agent according to underlying substrate and comorbid conditions.
For patients with AF in whom measuring serum digoxin levels is indicated, it is reasonable to target levels <1.2 ng/mL.

In patients with AF and HF symptoms, digoxin is reasonable for long-term rate control in combination with other rate-controlling agents, or as monotherapy if other agents are not preferred, not tolerated, or contraindicated.
In patients with AF and LVEF <40%, nondihydropyridine calcium channel-blocking drugs should not be administered given their potential to exacerbate HF.
In patients with permanent AF who have risk factors for cardiovascular events, dronedarone should not be used for long-term rate control.

content.Synopsis: Both nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (verapamil and diltiazem) and beta blockers are effective for long-term rate control. These agents are useful in ventricular rate control in the absence of preexcitation. Digoxin may be useful in patients with limited tolerability to other agents, or as adjunct therapy in patients with a difficult to control ventricular rate. Limited data exist comparing various rate control agents, especially in the setting of chronic rate control. Selection of specific agents should consider patient-specific characteristics (ie, HFrEF, reactive airway disease) and response.

content.Recommendation 1: A study compared four heart rate-reducing drug regimens on ventricular heart rate and arrhythmia-related symptoms in patients with permanent AF. Drugs used were diltiazem, verapamil, metoprolol, and carvedilol, all of which significantly reduced heart rate compared to baseline.
content.Recommendation 2: Investigators identified AF cases using emergency department data to find associations with hospitalization and mortality. Digoxin toxicity was found using electrocardiographic abnormalities. Higher serum concentrations of digoxin are linked with increased mortality.
content.Recommendation 3: Digoxin showed added efficacy when used with beta blockers in AF patients. A meta-analysis indicated an increased risk of death with digoxin, although some subanalyses did not observe increased mortality.
content.Recommendation 4: MDPIT study suggested worsening heart failure in patients with recent MI when treated with diltiazem, with placebo comparisons showing significant results. Diltiazem was associated with a rise in congestive heart failure frequency in low ejection fraction patients.
content.Recommendation 5: Dronedarone is designed to maintain sinus rhythm in AF patients and was found to reduce vascular events in permanent AF through trials like PALLAS. Benefits were suggested by ERATO trials.

content.title: 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
content.synopsis: AVNA provides effective ventricular rate control without medications but increases dependence on pacing. Important considerations include lifelong pacemaker implantation, especially regarding age and comorbidities. Small RCTs show AVNA and right ventricular pacing improve symptoms and QOL but do not affect EF or cardiac performance. AVNA results in permanent AF but does not improve quality of life with antiarrhythmic drugs. Risks include device-related complications, sudden cardiac death, and worsened heart failure, associated with malignant arrhythmias alleviated by early postprocedure rate programming. AVNA should not be a first-line therapy.

recommendations.Recommendation: In patients with AF and a persistently rapid ventricular response who undergo AVNA, initial pacemaker lower rate programming should be 80 to 90 bpm to reduce the risk of sudden death.
In patients with AF and uncontrolled rapid ventricular response refractory to rate-control medications, AVNA can be used to improve symptoms and quality of life.
In patients with AF who are planned to undergo AVNA, implantation of a pacemaker before ablation is recommended.
In patients with AF with normal EF undergoing AVNA, conduction system pacing of the His bundle or left bundle area may be reasonable.

Clinical Information: The risk of pacemaker-mediated cardiomyopathy and long-term follow-up data on pacing complications.
Risk of lead displacement or failure is approximately 2%.
AVNA and pacemaker implants have complication rates with displacements occurring in 3.3% within the first 2 months of implant.
Older patients undergoing AVNA have higher risks of complications.
HF after AVNA is linked to effects of RVP and baseline cardiac function.

Rhythm control goals for patients with different AF and HF conditions are outlined, suggesting rhythm control for patients with reduced LV function and persistent AF, symptomatic AF, AF diagnosis within 1 year, and improvement for HF and ischemia outcomes.
Pacing Techniques: His bundle and left bundle pacing are promising for reducing RVP-induced cardiomyopathy.
His bundle pacing is feasible and beneficial for patients with EF <40% and NYHA class.
Early studies showed stable EF with conduction system pacing.

synopsis.decision_making: When caring for a patient with AF, deciding between rhythm and rate control is important and can evolve over time.
synopsis.strategies: These two strategies are not mutually exclusive and depend on the specific clinical context.
synopsis.example_study: In EAST-AFNET 4, patients with early AF showed benefits from rhythm control in terms of reduced mortality, stroke, and hospitalization rates.

AF_and_LV_function.association: AF and reduced LV function are seen together with worse outcomes associated with patients with HF or AF alone.
AF_and_LV_function.LV_improvement: Several studies show LV function improvement after sinus rhythm restoration.

rhythm_vs_rate_control.effect: Rhythm and rate control improve symptoms in AF, with rhythm control often associated with better outcomes.

rhythm_control.findings: EAST-AFNET 4 shows rhythm control is beneficial in early AF.
rhythm_control.comparison: In AF-CHF, rhythm control did not affect cardiovascular mortality but improved quality of life.

content.figure_title: Patient and Clinical Considerations for Choosing Between Rhythm Control and Rate Control
additional_notes: Benefit of rhythm control in patients with HF is likely greatest in those with recently identified AF.
AF commonly, but not always, progresses over time to higher burdens and becomes more sustained.
Large registry studies have reported that AF progression is more commonly observed with a rate-control strategy compared to a rhythm-control strategy over a 1- to 2-year period.

content.Clinical Topic: 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation
content.Summary: The guidelines emphasize the importance of rhythm control in atrial fibrillation, suggesting both electrical and pharmacological cardioversion as effective methods. Electrical cardioversion is highlighted as more effective for patients with hemodynamic instability. Recommendations for preventing thromboembolism during cardioversion are also provided, with guidelines on anticoagulation therapy.

Key Recommendations.Description: In patients with AF duration of ≥48 hours, a 3-week duration of uninterrupted therapeutic anticoagulation or imaging evaluation to exclude intracardiac thrombus is recommended before elective cardioversion.

In patients with AF undergoing cardioversion, therapeutic anticoagulation should be established before cardioversion and continued for at least 4 weeks afterwards without interruption to prevent thromboembolism.

In patients with AF in whom cardioversion is deferred due to LAA thrombus detected on precardioversion imaging, therapeutic anticoagulation should be instituted for at least 3 to 6 weeks, after which imaging should be repeated before cardioversion.

content.Additional Notes:
- Cardioversion is crucial for acute rhythm control in patients, especially with hemodynamic instability due to AF.
- Electrical cardioversion, though requiring anesthesia or sedation, is emphasized for its efficacy.
- The importance of anticoagulation in reducing thromboembolism risk is reiterated.

content.Synopsis: Thromboembolic risks and considerations of anticoagulation apply to both pharmacological cardioversion and electrical cardioversion. Rapid recovery of mechanical atrial systole post-cardioversion may influence clinical variables and the duration of atrial stunning. Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) are preferred over vitamin K antagonists (VKAs) for patients with atrial fibrillation (AF) undergoing cardioversion, with exceptions. Precautionary imaging is suggested for patients with previously detected thrombi even under anticoagulation. Inadequate occlusion of devices like LAAO may appear early in the procedure or delayed.

Thromboembolic events are uncommon within 6 weeks post-cardioversion.

The safety of cardioversion without prior anticoagulation assessment for AF duration <48 hours has been debated.
Time to cardioversion >12 hours is an independent predictor for thromboembolic complications.
Retrospective studies demonstrate low thromboembolic risks with certain CHA2DS2-VASc scores.
Recommendations: Immediate electrical cardioversion is advised for hemodynamically unstable AF patients to restore rhythm.
For stable AF patients, electrical cardioversion can be used as an initial strategy or after pharmacological attempts.
Synchronized energy delivery is recommended during electrical cardioversion.
For elective electrical cardioversion, using biphasic energy of at least 200 J may improve success.
Increased AF duration or unsuccessful initial shocks may benefit from higher energy or premedication with antiarrhythmic drugs.
In AF patients with obesity, manual pressure augmentation during energy escalation can enhance outcomes.

key_points: In patients with hemodynamically unstable AF, immediate electrical cardioversion with synchronized direct current is preferred.
Electrical cardioversion achieves higher success rates of restoring sinus rhythm compared to pharmacological alone.
Goals of rhythm control include effective and safe management with both electrical and pharmacological cardioversion.
For patients with unstable AF, immediate electrical and rhythmic synchronization are critical.
Data supports that electrical cardioversion is effective, especially when timing and proper synchronization are ensured.
Clinical trials show the efficacy of electrical cardioversion with differentiated energy delivery strategies.
recommendations: Consider electrical over pharmacological cardioversion when sedation is tolerable.
Ensure monitoring for rare chronotropic or thromboembolic complications during cardioversion.
Synchronization with the QRS complex is crucial in electrical cardioversion.
